title: Some customer phone numbers are wrong in TaskFlow
body: We noticed that some of our customer contact records have incorrect phone numbers. The numbers look truncated or mangled. Our support team caught it because they tried calling a few customers and got wrong numbers. We have about 2,000 customer records and I'd estimate maybe 50-100 have wrong numbers. This is a serious data integrity issue for us.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for flagging this — data integrity issues like this are definitely something we want to get to the bottom of.

To help me understand the full picture: can you tell me more about how these customer phone numbers get into TaskFlow in the first place? For example, are they entered manually by your team, imported from a spreadsheet or another system, or synced automatically from somewhere? And do you have any sense of whether these numbers were ever correct in TaskFlow and became wrong at some point, or whether they may have been wrong from the moment they were added?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: They come from our CRM — we use Salesforce — through a nightly batch import that syncs everything automatically. And yeah, I'm pretty sure they were correct at some point. We've been using this setup for a while and only started noticing the bad numbers recently, maybe in the last couple weeks. Our support team was calling customers they've called before and suddenly the numbers were wrong, so they definitely changed at some point.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's really helpful context — knowing the numbers were correct before and changed recently narrows things down a lot.

I'd love to follow that thread a bit further: can you tell me more about what "truncated or mangled" looks like in practice? For example, if a correct number was `(555) 123-4567`, what does the wrong version look like now — is it missing digits, missing the area code, has extra characters, or something else entirely? If you could share a couple of before/after examples (with fake numbers if needed for privacy), that would be incredibly useful.

Also, do you recall whether anything changed around two weeks ago — either on the TaskFlow side (an update, a settings change) or on the Salesforce side (field changes, a new integration, phone number format updates)?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
So looking at a few examples, it seems like the country code part is getting cut short. Like we have customers in the Caribbean and Ireland — one number that should start with +1268 now just shows +12, and an Irish number that was +353 is now +35. The rest of the number after the country code looks okay, it's just the country code itself that's getting chopped. Interestingly our US and UK customers seem fine — their +1 and +44 numbers look correct.

As for what changed two weeks ago, I'm not sure on the TaskFlow side — I don't manage updates myself. Nothing changed on our Salesforce side that I'm aware of. The numbers are still correct in Salesforce, it's only in TaskFlow where they're wrong. So it feels like something in the sync process maybe?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
This is a great find — the pattern you've described is very telling. It looks like country codes are being truncated to exactly 2 characters, which would explain why US (+1) and UK (+44) numbers survive but longer codes like +353 or +1268 don't.

One thing I'd like to understand before we hand this off to the team: since the nightly sync runs every night, do you know if numbers that get corrected manually in TaskFlow stay fixed, or do they get re-corrupted the next morning when the sync runs again? That would tell us whether the sync itself is actively mangling the numbers on every run, or whether this was a one-time issue that happened and the sync is now just preserving the bad data.

Also, if you happen to have a sense of the specific date when your support team first noticed the wrong numbers, that would help us pinpoint exactly which change might have introduced this.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
I haven't tried manually correcting any numbers in TaskFlow honestly, so I can't say for sure whether they'd get overwritten the next night. But based on how the sync works, I'd assume it would just overwrite them again since it pulls fresh data from Salesforce each time. The Salesforce numbers are correct though, so... actually I'm not sure what would happen. It might depend on whether the mangling happens during the import step.

As for when it was first noticed — I think our support team started complaining about it maybe a week and a half ago? So the issue probably started a few days before that. I'd guess roughly two weeks ago give or take.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Nightly Salesforce sync truncates phone number country codes to 2 characters","problem":"Customer phone numbers imported from Salesforce via the nightly batch sync are having their country codes truncated to exactly 2 characters. Numbers with country codes longer than 2 digits (e.g., +353 for Ireland, +1268 for Antigua) are corrupted, while numbers with 1-2 digit country codes (+1 for US, +44 for UK) appear unaffected. Approximately 50-100 of ~2,000 customer records are affected. The source data in Salesforce remains correct.","root_cause_hypothesis":"A change introduced approximately two weeks ago in the phone number parsing or storage logic of the Salesforce sync/import pipeline is truncating the country code portion of phone numbers to a maximum of 2 characters. This could be a database column width change, a substring/parsing bug in the import code, or a new validation rule that silently truncates. The fact that the rest of the number after the country code is preserved suggests the truncation targets the country code field or parsing segment specifically, not the overall number length.","reproduction_steps":["Identify a customer record with a country code longer than 2 digits (e.g., an Irish number with +353) that is correct in Salesforce","Wait for or manually trigger the nightly batch sync from Salesforce to TaskFlow","Examine the phone number for that record in TaskFlow — the country code should be truncated to 2 characters (e.g., +353 becomes +35)","Verify that a record with a 1-2 digit country code (e.g., +1 or +44) is not affected"],"environment":"Production TaskFlow instance with nightly batch import from Salesforce CRM. Issue affects the sync/import pipeline, not the Salesforce source data.","severity":"high","impact":"Support team cannot reach customers with affected phone numbers. Approximately 50-100 records are corrupted, specifically international customers with country codes longer than 2 digits. Data integrity of customer contact information is compromised and likely worsening with each nightly sync if the mangling is applied on every run.","recommended_fix":"1. Review git history for the Salesforce sync/import code for changes made approximately 2-3 weeks ago. 2. Look specifically at phone number parsing logic — check for any substring, column width, or validation change that would cap the country code at 2 characters. 3. Check whether the database schema for the phone number or country code field was altered. 4. Fix the truncation bug and re-sync affected records from Salesforce to restore correct numbers. 5. Consider adding a validation check that flags phone numbers whose length changed during import.","proposed_test_case":"Create test records with phone numbers using country codes of varying lengths: +1 (1 digit), +44 (2 digits), +353 (3 digits), +1268 (4 digits). Run the import/sync logic and assert that all country codes are preserved in full. Additionally, regression-test against a snapshot of known-good Salesforce data to ensure no truncation occurs.","information_gaps":["Exact date the issue was introduced (reporter estimates ~2 weeks ago; sync logs or git history should pinpoint this)","Whether the sync re-corrupts numbers on every run or if this was a one-time corruption (testable by the developer)","Whether a specific TaskFlow update or migration was deployed around that time"]}
